name: Measure Words
summary: Chinese counts nouns with a measure word between the number and the noun.
explanation:
In Chinese you cannot say 'three books' directly. You need a measure word (量词) between the number and the noun: 三本书 (sān běn shū) = three (volume) books. English does this only sometimes (a sheet of paper, two cups of tea); Chinese does it every time.

Different nouns take different measure words. 本 (běn) is for books, 个 (ge) is the general all-purpose one, 杯 (bēi) is for cups, 张 (zhāng) is for flat things like paper and tickets, and 只 (zhī) is for many animals.

When in doubt, 个 (ge) is understood almost everywhere, so beginners can lean on it while learning the specific ones.

examples.en: three cups of coffee (杯 for cups)
This apple is big. (个 with 这, this)

commonMistakes: Dropping the measure word: it is 三本书, not 三书.
Using 二 instead of 两 before a measure word: it is 两个 (liǎng ge), not 二个.
Forgetting the measure word after 这 (this) and 那 (that): it is 这个, not 这.
Using the wrong measure word. When unsure, the general 个 is usually accepted.
Putting the measure word in the wrong place. It always sits between the number and the noun.
tip: Learn each new noun together with its measure word, the way you learned der/die/das in German. Default to 个 when you are stuck.
